{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社}} | image = Nishinomiya-jinja02st3200.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|44|8.62|N|135|20|4.48|E|region:JP_source:jawiki_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} '' main hall]] '''{{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社|Nishinomiya jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] in [[Nishinomiya, Hyōgo|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the head shrine of the [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] sect of Shinto, and it is said that there are about 3,500 shrines under it. Locals call the shrine "Ebessan".
==History== It is not clear when this shrine was established. However, it is recorded that it was already on this site, under the name Ebisu-sha, and attracting many worshipers during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. For many centuries it was known as Nangu-sha, the "Southern Shrine", in reference to its status as a branch shrine of [[Hirota Shrine]], which is located to its north in Nishinomiya. Nishinomiya Shrine itself had a similar relationship with [[Koshikiiwa Shrine]], which was sometimes called Kita no Ebisu, meaning the Northern Ebisu.
==Objects of worship== Nishinomiya Shrine has three small inner shrines and each shrine covers one or two [[kami]]. The first inner shrine covers Nishinomiya-Ōkami, or Ebisu-no-mikoto, namely Ebisu. The kami of the second shrine are [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]] and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushino-Mikoto]]. The kami of third shrine is [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].
It is famous for the Tōka-Ebisu festival, which is held on January 10 every year. Particular to this festival is the "Lucky Men" race. Begun during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. The top three finishers are given the title of "Lucky Men", and of those three the champion is known as the "Luckiest Man". The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>
==Access== *[[Nishinomiya Station (Hanshin)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:Hanshin Main Line|Hanshin Main Line]] *[[Nishinomiya Station (JR West)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:JR Kobe Line|JR Kobe Line]]
==References== <references />
==External links== {{Commonscat-inline}} *[http://www.decca-japan.com/nishinomiya_ebisu/ Nishinomiya Shrine Homepage] (in Japanese) *[http://www.geocities.co.jp/SilkRoad-Forest/2991/ebisu.html Nishinomiya Shrine's biggest festival "Tokaebisu" Homepage] (in Japanese, one of lecturer of Kitakyushu College of Technology wrote) *[http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do;jsessionid=0DB7487CDBFFB0299115D5BE791F9818?class_name=col_jat&data_id=75436 Thesis of Historical transition of TOKA-EBISU "Open Gate" Ceremony in Nishinomiya Shinto shrine] (in English, Hironori Arakawa, Associate Professor of National Institute of Technology, Akashi College wrote)

==Access== *[[Nishinomiya Station (Hanshin)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:Hanshin Main Line|Hanshin Main Line]] *[[Nishinomiya Station (JR West)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:JR Kobe Line|JR Kobe Line]]

For many centuries it was known as Nangu-sha, the "Southern Shrine", in reference to its status as a branch shrine of [[Hirota Shrine]], which is located to its north in Nishinomiya. Nishinomiya Shrine itself had a similar relationship with [[Koshikiiwa Shrine]], which was sometimes called Kita no Ebisu, meaning the Northern Ebisu.
==Objects of worship== Nishinomiya Shrine has three small inner shrines and each shrine covers one or two [[kami]]. The first inner shrine covers Nishinomiya-Ōkami, or Ebisu-no-mikoto, namely Ebisu. The kami of the second shrine are [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]] and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushino-Mikoto]]. The kami of third shrine is [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].
It is famous for the Tōka-Ebisu festival, which is held on January 10 every year. Particular to this festival is the "Lucky Men" race. Begun during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. The top three finishers are given the title of "Lucky Men", and of those three the champion is known as the "Luckiest Man". The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>
==Access== *[[Nishinomiya Station (Hanshin)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:Hanshin Main Line|Hanshin Main Line]] *[[Nishinomiya Station (JR West)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:JR Kobe Line|JR Kobe Line]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q705297]] Wikidata name: Nishinomiya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社}} | image = File:Nishinomiya-jinja01s4592.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73572778|N|135.33457778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602}}; {{ill|Ebisu|qid=Q1129330}}; {{ill|Susanoo|qid=Q272993}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|qid=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290}}; {{ill|Nishinomiya|qid=Q243853}} }} '' main hall]] '''{{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社|Nishinomiya jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the head shrine of the [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] sect of Shinto, and it is said that there are about 3,500 shrines under it. Locals call the shrine "Ebessan".
==History== It is not clear when this shrine was established. However, it is recorded that it was already on this site, under the name Ebisu-sha, and attracting many worshipers during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. For many centuries it was known as Nangu-sha, the "Southern Shrine", in reference to its status as a branch shrine of [[Hirota Shrine]], which is located to its north in Nishinomiya. Nishinomiya Shrine itself had a similar relationship with [[Koshikiiwa Shrine]], which was sometimes called Kita no Ebisu, meaning the Northern Ebisu.
==Objects of worship== Nishinomiya Shrine has three small inner shrines and each shrine covers one or two [[kami]]. The first inner shrine covers Nishinomiya-Ōkami, or Ebisu-no-mikoto, namely Ebisu. The kami of the second shrine are [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]] and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushino-Mikoto]]. The kami of third shrine is [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].
It is famous for the Tōka-Ebisu festival, which is held on January 10 every year. Particular to this festival is the "Lucky Men" race. Begun during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. The top three finishers are given the title of "Lucky Men", and of those three the champion is known as the "Luckiest Man". The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>
==Access== *[[Nishinomiya Station (Hanshin)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:Hanshin Main Line|Hanshin Main Line]] *[[Nishinomiya Station (JR West)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:JR Kobe Line|JR Kobe Line]]

It is famous for the Tōka-Ebisu festival, which is held on January 10 every year. Particular to this festival is the "Lucky Men" race. Begun during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. The top three finishers are given the title of "Lucky Men", and of those three the champion is known as the "Luckiest Man". The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>

Begun during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q705297]] Wikidata name: Nishinomiya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社}} | image = File:Nishinomiya-jinja01s4592.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73572778|N|135.33457778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Ebisu|WD=Q1129330}}; {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290}}; {{ill|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853}} }} '' main hall]] '''{{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社|Nishinomiya jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the head shrine of the [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] sect of Shinto, and it is said that there are about 3,500 shrines under it. Locals call the shrine "Ebessan".
==History== It is not clear when this shrine was established. However, it is recorded that it was already on this site, under the name Ebisu-sha, and attracting many worshipers during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. For many centuries it was known as Nangu-sha, the "Southern Shrine", in reference to its status as a branch shrine of [[Hirota Shrine]], which is located to its north in Nishinomiya. Nishinomiya Shrine itself had a similar relationship with [[Koshikiiwa Shrine]], which was sometimes called Kita no Ebisu, meaning the Northern Ebisu.
==Objects of worship== Nishinomiya Shrine has three small inner shrines and each shrine covers one or two [[kami]]. The first inner shrine covers Nishinomiya-Ōkami, or Ebisu-no-mikoto, namely Ebisu. The kami of the second shrine are [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]] and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushino-Mikoto]]. The kami of third shrine is [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].
It is famous for the Tōka-Ebisu festival, which is held on January 10 every year. Particular to this festival is the "Lucky Men" race. Begun during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. The top three finishers are given the title of "Lucky Men", and of those three the champion is known as the "Luckiest Man". The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>
==Access== *[[Nishinomiya Station (Hanshin)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:Hanshin Main Line|Hanshin Main Line]] *[[Nishinomiya Station (JR West)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:JR Kobe Line|JR Kobe Line]]
==References== <references />
==External links== {{Commonscat-inline}} *[http://www.decca-japan.com/nishinomiya_ebisu/ Nishinomiya Shrine Homepage] (in Japanese) *[http://www.geocities.co.jp/SilkRoad-Forest/2991/ebisu.html Nishinomiya Shrine's biggest festival "Tokaebisu" Homepage] (in Japanese, one of lecturer of Kitakyushu College of Technology wrote) *[http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do;jsessionid=0DB7487CDBFFB0299115D5BE791F9818?class_name=col_jat&data_id=75436 Thesis of Historical transition of TOKA-EBISU "Open Gate" Ceremony in Nishinomiya Shinto shrine] (in English, Hironori Arakawa, Associate Professor of National Institute of Technology, Akashi College wrote)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q705297]] Wikidata name: Nishinomiya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社}} | image = File:Nishinomiya-jinja01s4592.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73572778|N|135.33457778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Ebisu|WD=Q1129330}}; {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290}}; {{ill|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853}} }} '' main hall]] '''{{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社|Nishinomiya jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] in [[:en:Nishinomiya|Nishinomiya]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the head shrine of the [[:en:Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]] sect of Shinto, and it is said that there are about 3,500 shrines under it. Locals call the shrine "Ebessan".
==History== It is not clear when this shrine was established. However, it is recorded that it was already on this site, under the name Ebisu-sha, and attracting many worshipers during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. For many centuries it was known as Nangu-sha, the "Southern Shrine", in reference to its status as a branch shrine of [[Hirota Shrine]], which is located to its north in Nishinomiya. Nishinomiya Shrine itself had a similar relationship with [[Koshikiiwa Shrine]], which was sometimes called Kita no Ebisu, meaning the Northern Ebisu.
==Objects of worship== Nishinomiya Shrine has three small inner shrines and each shrine covers one or two [[kami]]. The first inner shrine covers Nishinomiya-Ōkami, or Ebisu-no-mikoto, namely Ebisu. The kami of the second shrine are [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]] and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushino-Mikoto]]. The kami of third shrine is [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].
It is famous for the Tōka-Ebisu festival, which is held on January 10 every year. Particular to this festival is the "Lucky Men" race. Begun during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. The top three finishers are given the title of "Lucky Men", and of those three the champion is known as the "Luckiest Man". The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q705297]] Wikidata name: Nishinomiya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社}} | image = File:Nishinomiya-jinja01s4592.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73572778|N|135.33457778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Ebisu|WD=Q1129330}}; {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290}}; {{ill|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853}} }} '' main hall]] '''{{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社|Nishinomiya jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|en|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853|lt=Nishinomiya}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the head shrine of the {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu}} sect of Shinto, and it is said that there are about 3,500 shrines under it. Locals call the shrine "Ebessan".
==History== It is not clear when this shrine was established. However, it is recorded that it was already on this site, under the name Ebisu-sha, and attracting many worshipers during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. For many centuries it was known as Nangu-sha, the "Southern Shrine", in reference to its status as a branch shrine of [[Hirota Shrine]], which is located to its north in Nishinomiya. Nishinomiya Shrine itself had a similar relationship with [[Koshikiiwa Shrine]], which was sometimes called Kita no Ebisu, meaning the Northern Ebisu.
==Objects of worship== Nishinomiya Shrine has three small inner shrines and each shrine covers one or two [[kami]]. The first inner shrine covers Nishinomiya-Ōkami, or Ebisu-no-mikoto, namely Ebisu. The kami of the second shrine are [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]] and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushino-Mikoto]]. The kami of third shrine is [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].
It is famous for the Tōka-Ebisu festival, which is held on January 10 every year. Particular to this festival is the "Lucky Men" race. Begun during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. The top three finishers are given the title of "Lucky Men", and of those three the champion is known as the "Luckiest Man". The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>
==Access== *[[Nishinomiya Station (Hanshin)|Nishinomiya Station]] of {{ill|Hanshin Main Line|en|Hanshin Main Line|WD=Q1053647|lt=Hanshin Main Line}} *[[Nishinomiya Station (JR West)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:JR Kobe Line|JR Kobe Line]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q705297]] Wikidata name: Nishinomiya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社}} | image = File:Nishinomiya-jinja01s4592.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73572778|N|135.33457778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Ebisu|WD=Q1129330}}; {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290}}; {{ill|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853}} }} '' main hall]] '''{{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社|Nishinomiya jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|en|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853|lt=Nishinomiya}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the head shrine of the {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu}} sect of Shinto, and it is said that there are about 3,500 shrines under it. Locals call the shrine "Ebessan".
==History== It is not clear when this shrine was established. However, it is recorded that it was already on this site, under the name Ebisu-sha, and attracting many worshipers during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. For many centuries it was known as Nangu-sha, the "Southern Shrine", in reference to its status as a branch shrine of [[Hirota Shrine]], which is located to its north in Nishinomiya. Nishinomiya Shrine itself had a similar relationship with [[Koshikiiwa Shrine]], which was sometimes called Kita no Ebisu, meaning the Northern Ebisu.
==Objects of worship== Nishinomiya Shrine has three small inner shrines and each shrine covers one or two [[kami]]. The first inner shrine covers Nishinomiya-Ōkami, or Ebisu-no-mikoto, namely Ebisu. The kami of the second shrine are [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]] and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushino-Mikoto]]. The kami of third shrine is [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].
It is famous for the Tōka-Ebisu festival, which is held on January 10 every year. Particular to this festival is the "Lucky Men" race. Begun during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. The top three finishers are given the title of "Lucky Men", and of those three the champion is known as the "Luckiest Man". The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>
==Access== *[[Nishinomiya Station (Hanshin)|Nishinomiya Station]] of {{ill|Hanshin Main Line|en|Hanshin Main Line|WD=Q1053647|lt=Hanshin Main Line}} *[[Nishinomiya Station (JR West)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:JR Kobe Line|JR Kobe Line]]

==Objects of worship== Nishinomiya Shrine has three small inner shrines and each shrine covers one or two [[kami]]. The first inner shrine covers Nishinomiya-Ōkami, or Ebisu-no-mikoto, namely Ebisu. The kami of the second shrine are [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]] and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushino-Mikoto]]. The kami of third shrine is [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].
It is famous for the Tōka-Ebisu festival, which is held on January 10 every year. Particular to this festival is the "Lucky Men" race. Begun during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. The top three finishers are given the title of "Lucky Men", and of those three the champion is known as the "Luckiest Man". The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>

It is the head shrine of the {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu}} sect of Shinto, and it is said that there are about 3,500 shrines under it. Locals call the shrine "Ebessan".

The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社}} | image = File:Nishinomiya-jinja01s4592.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73572778|N|135.33457778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Ebisu|WD=Q1129330}}; {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290}}; {{ill|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853}} }} '' main hall]] '''{{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社|Nishinomiya jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|en|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853|lt=Nishinomiya}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the head shrine of the {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu}} sect of Shinto, and it is said that there are about 3,500 shrines under it. Locals call the shrine "Ebessan".
==History== It is not clear when this shrine was established. However, it is recorded that it was already on this site, under the name Ebisu-sha, and attracting many worshipers during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. For many centuries it was known as Nangu-sha, the "Southern Shrine", in reference to its status as a branch shrine of [[Hirota Shrine]], which is located to its north in Nishinomiya. Nishinomiya Shrine itself had a similar relationship with [[Koshikiiwa Shrine]], which was sometimes called Kita no Ebisu, meaning the Northern Ebisu.
==Objects of worship== Nishinomiya Shrine has three small inner shrines and each shrine covers one or two [[kami]]. The first inner shrine covers Nishinomiya-Ōkami, or Ebisu-no-mikoto, namely Ebisu. The kami of the second shrine are [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]] and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushino-Mikoto]]. The kami of third shrine is [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].
It is famous for the Tōka-Ebisu festival, which is held on January 10 every year. Particular to this festival is the "Lucky Men" race. Begun during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. The top three finishers are given the title of "Lucky Men", and of those three the champion is known as the "Luckiest Man". The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>
==Access== *[[Nishinomiya Station (Hanshin)|Nishinomiya Station]] of {{ill|Hanshin Main Line|en|Hanshin Main Line|WD=Q1053647|lt=Hanshin Main Line}} *[[Nishinomiya Station (JR West)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:JR Kobe Line|JR Kobe Line]]
==References== <references />
==External links== {{Commonscat-inline}} *[http://www.decca-japan.com/nishinomiya_ebisu/ Nishinomiya Shrine Homepage] (in Japanese) *[http://www.geocities.co.jp/SilkRoad-Forest/2991/ebisu.html Nishinomiya Shrine's biggest festival "Tokaebisu" Homepage] (in Japanese, one of lecturer of Kitakyushu College of Technology wrote) *[http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do;jsessionid=0DB7487CDBFFB0299115D5BE791F9818?class_name=col_jat&data_id=75436 Thesis of Historical transition of TOKA-EBISU "Open Gate" Ceremony in Nishinomiya Shinto shrine] (in English, Hironori Arakawa, Associate Professor of National Institute of Technology, Akashi College wrote)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[de:Nishinomiya-Schrein]][[en:Nishinomiya Shrine]][[es:Santuario Nishinomiya]][[fr:Nishinomiya-jinja]][[ja:西宮神社]][[ru:Храм Нисиномия]][[zh:西宮神社]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社}} | image = File:Nishinomiya-jinja01s4592.jpg | caption = ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.73572778|N|135.33457778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Ebisu|WD=Q1129330|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}; {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}; {{ill|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853|1=Nishinomiya}} }} '' main hall]] '''{{nihongo|Nishinomiya Shrine|西宮神社|Nishinomiya jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Nishinomiya|en|Nishinomiya|WD=Q243853|lt=Nishinomiya|1=Nishinomiya}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is the head shrine of the {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} sect of Shinto, and it is said that there are about 3,500 shrines under it. Locals call the shrine "Ebessan".
==History== It is not clear when this shrine was established. However, it is recorded that it was already on this site, under the name Ebisu-sha, and attracting many worshipers during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. For many centuries it was known as Nangu-sha, the "Southern Shrine", in reference to its status as a branch shrine of [[Hirota Shrine]], which is located to its north in Nishinomiya. Nishinomiya Shrine itself had a similar relationship with [[Koshikiiwa Shrine]], which was sometimes called Kita no Ebisu, meaning the Northern Ebisu.
==Objects of worship== Nishinomiya Shrine has three small inner shrines and each shrine covers one or two [[kami]]. The first inner shrine covers Nishinomiya-Ōkami, or Ebisu-no-mikoto, namely Ebisu. The kami of the second shrine are [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]] and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushino-Mikoto]]. The kami of third shrine is [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].
It is famous for the Tōka-Ebisu festival, which is held on January 10 every year. Particular to this festival is the "Lucky Men" race. Begun during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, participants gather in front of the shrine's main gate before 6 am on 10 January. At 6 am, the shrine's drum sounds, the gates are opened, and the assembled crowd sprints perilously 230 meters to the main hall. The top three finishers are given the title of "Lucky Men", and of those three the champion is known as the "Luckiest Man". The race has been known to attract more than 6,000 runners.<ref>{{Citation| url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20100111a4.html|title=Track runner wins 'lucky man' race|publisher=[[The Japan Times]]|accessdate=10 January 2010 }}.</ref>
==Access== *[[Nishinomiya Station (Hanshin)|Nishinomiya Station]] of {{ill|Hanshin Main Line|en|Hanshin Main Line|WD=Q1053647|lt=Hanshin Main Line|1=Hanshin Main Line}} *[[Nishinomiya Station (JR West)|Nishinomiya Station]] of [[:en:JR Kobe Line|JR Kobe Line]]
